title: The line without which a phone lies
checkLabels.t3: There is a line for the phone
checkLabels.t4: The line is in the head
checkLabels.t5: It tells the truth about the width
why.empty: The editor is empty. The task is one line — not deleting the page. Bring the code back.
why.untouched: You have not touched the code. The phone is still pretending to be a computer.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
why.lost-content: You deleted something instead of adding. The heading, the sentence, the charset, the tab title, the language — all of it stays. You are only ADDING one line.
hint2.lost-content: Compare with the original code. Nothing was supposed to disappear.
why.no-viewport: The line for the phone is missing. Without it a phone pretends to be a computer — it draws your page at 980 pixels and then shrinks it. The result: letters like ants.
hint2.no-viewport: Another <meta> tag. Like the one for the charset — invisible, but nothing is right without it.
hint3.no-viewport: <meta name="..." content="..."> — the name says what the tag is about, the content says exactly what.
why.outside-head: The tag is there, but not in the head. Meta tags go in <head>. In the body the browser sees them too late — the page is already drawn.
hint2.outside-head: Put it next to <meta charset>. The two always sit together.
why.no-width: The tag is there, but it does not say the important part. width=device-width means: the page's width is the device's width. Without it nothing changes — the phone keeps pretending.
hint2.no-width: The content of the tag is what does the work. An empty content says nothing.
hint3.no-width: Two things inside, separated by a comma. One is about the width. The other says do not zoom in or out.
blocks.0.text: Open your page on a phone. You will see something odd: it looks like the desktop version, only shrunk. Letters like ants. You have to pinch and zoom to read anything at all.
blocks.1.text: You did nothing wrong. The phone is lying to you — and there is a reason.
blocks.2.text: Why it pretends to be a computer
blocks.3.text: When phones got good enough for the web, the sites already existed. Millions of them, all written for wide screens.
blocks.4.text: If a phone had shown such a site at its true width, it would have fallen apart. So they decided: I will pretend to be a 980-pixel screen, draw the page that way, and then shrink the whole thing to fit.
blocks.5.text: Hence the ants. The site is drawn for a wide screen and then shrunk — not rewritten for a narrow one.
blocks.8.text: Another <meta> in the head. width=device-width means: the page's width is the device's width. No pretending.
blocks.9.text: initial-scale=1 means: do not zoom in or out. Show me as I am.
blocks.10.text: And notice where it goes: into <head>, next to the charset. In the body it does nothing — there the browser sees it after the page is already drawn.
blocks.12.text: This line tells the phone not to lie. It does not make your site look good on a narrow screen.
blocks.13.text: If you have a table with eight columns, it will run off the edge. If your image is 2000 pixels, it will stick out. That is CSS's job.
blocks.14.text: But without this line CSS never even starts — the phone does not believe it, because it is pretending to be another screen.
blocks.16.text: Google looks at sites the way they appear on a PHONE. Not a computer. If yours shows up as a shrunken desktop version, it falls in the results.
blocks.17.text: And more than half the people will open your site on a phone. Not one day — now.
blocks.18.text: One line in the head. Without it half your visitors see ants.
blocks.19.text: Write it out of habit, like charset. The two of them belong in the head of every page you will ever build.
blocks.20.text: Your turn. Tell the phone the truth.
blocks.21.text: And if you have a phone to hand — open the page on it before and after. The difference is obvious.
